name: azure
context: Microsoft Azure cloud ecosystem for resource automation, hosting, storage, secrets, serverless, monitoring, and Kubernetes operations
info: Azure is Microsoft's ARM-based cloud control plane. Autonomous agents use it to authenticate with Microsoft Entra ID, select the correct tenant and subscription, create or inspect resource groups, provision storage and secret dependencies, deploy web and serverless workloads, attach identities, and bootstrap AKS clusters. The dominant execution surface is the `az` CLI, backed by subscription-scoped RBAC and resource IDs. This tree is organized around the recurring jobs an engineering agent actually performs: set auth context, manage resource groups, move artifacts through storage, deploy App Service and Function Apps, protect credentials in Key Vault, reach Kubernetes clusters through AKS, and inspect incidents with Azure Monitor.
triggers: deploy an app service to azure
store a secret in azure key vault
body:
# Azure Ecosystem

Azure automation is built around three facts that agents must keep straight at all times:

1. Authentication is tenant-scoped and enforced by Microsoft Entra ID.
2. Almost every resource lives inside a subscription and a resource group.
3. The fastest operational surface is usually the Azure CLI, not the portal.

~~~bash
az login
az account show
az account set --subscription "$AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID"
az configure --defaults group=rg-dojo location=eastus
az group create --name rg-dojo --location eastus
az resource list --resource-group rg-dojo --output table
~~~

For autonomous agents, the safest pattern is: authenticate with [[azure/auth]], confirm the active subscription, create or inspect [[azure/resource-groups]], provision dependencies like [[azure/storage]] and [[azure/key-vault]], then deploy workloads with [[azure/app-service]], [[azure/functions]], or [[azure/aks]]. After any rollout or failure, jump to [[azure/monitor]].

Azure CLI defaults are sticky. `az configure --defaults` writes profile defaults, and `az webapp up` also caches deployment parameters in `.azure/config` inside the project directory. That accelerates repeat execution, but it also means an agent must re-check subscription, group, and location before destructive changes.

The other durable mental model is ARM identity: every resource has a fully qualified resource ID such as `/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/<rg>/providers/Microsoft.Web/sites/<name>`. Those IDs become the hand-off format between deployment, monitoring, RBAC, and downstream tools.

Core paths in this ecosystem:

- [[azure/auth]] for user, service principal, and managed identity login
- [[azure/resource-groups]] for blast-radius boundaries and lifecycle management
- [[azure/storage]] for accounts, containers, and blob artifact movement
- [[azure/app-service]] for long-running web workloads
- [[azure/functions]] for serverless functions and zip deployments
- [[azure/key-vault]] for secrets and RBAC-protected secret operations
- [[azure/aks]] for managed Kubernetes clusters
- [[azure/monitor]] for metrics, activity logs, and Log Analytics queries

sections.title: Resource model
sections.body: Azure operations are usually subscription-scoped and resource-group scoped. Resource groups are the safest deletion and tagging boundary for most app environments, while ARM resource IDs are the canonical reference format for logs, metrics, RBAC scopes, and cross-resource wiring. Create or inspect the group first, then place dependent services inside it.

sections.title: Authentication and active context
sections.body: The active tenant and subscription matter as much as the command itself. Start with `az account show` and `az account set --subscription ...` before creating, deleting, or updating resources. Use [[azure/auth]] for the right noninteractive login pattern; service principals and managed identities are safer for automation than human interactive sessions.

sections.title: Agent workflows
sections.body: The recurring jobs are predictable: create a resource group, create storage for artifacts or function runtime state, store secrets in Key Vault, deploy a web or function artifact, attach identities or app settings, fetch AKS credentials when the workload is Kubernetes-based, and then inspect metrics or logs in [[azure/monitor]]. The shortest path depends on the runtime shape rather than the product marketing page.

links.context: Authenticate and pin the correct tenant and subscription context
Create and manage the boundary that most Azure resources live inside
Move artifacts and data through storage accounts and blob containers
Deploy long-running web apps with `az webapp`
Provision and deploy Azure Function Apps
Store and retrieve secrets with RBAC-aware workflows
Create clusters and fetch kubeconfig for managed Kubernetes
Inspect metrics, activity logs, and Log Analytics data
Model Azure infrastructure declaratively once the control-plane concepts are clear
Operate workloads after AKS hands off cluster credentials

related.note: AWS is the closest comparable public cloud control plane in the graph
GCP covers a similar cloud automation surface with different IAM and resource models
Many Azure teams provision the same services declaratively with Terraform
AKS is Azure's managed Kubernetes surface and hands off to generic Kubernetes operations

author: dojo-community
